coredata.dc:title: Samarium, nitrogen co-Doped carbon dots for detection of Epinephrine: Theoretical and experimental
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Duhan, Jyoti
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: In this study we have developed a highly sensitive optical sensor for the detection of epinephrine using carbon dots co-doped with samarium and nitrogen (Sm,N-CDs) with a very low detection limit of 28.1 nM and a broad linear dynamic range from (0–105 nM). Various techniques were employed to characterize the synthesized material, aiming to understand its morphological and physicochemical characteristics. The synergistic effect of the synthesized Sm,N-CDs demonstrated excellent optical performance, high selectivity and photostability. Additionally, good recovery findings were obtained when the sensor’s viability for Epinephrine detection in biological fluid samples that had been spiked was evaluated. In addition, we have developed an smartphones-based sensor to record the solution’s fluorescent color shift as it is being sensed. Using a mobile phone application to examine the green, red, and blue values from these photos, the comparable LOD was found to be 17.21 μM in a (0–90 μM) wide linear range of concentration. This straightforward, affordable, and quick screening device is highly demanding for the on-the-spot identification of the analytes at remote locations where advanced instrumentation is typically unavailable. Density functional theory was used to examine energy, stability, band gap, and how Ep interacted with the Sm,N-CDs nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inner filter effect
Fluorescence
Density functional theory
Carbon dots
Nanomaterials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Building the nanosensor based on Sm,N-CDs
Specificity and reliability for real analysis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image (b) FE-SEM image (c) AFM Image (d) XRD spectra (e) Average particle size (f) FT-IR spectra of synthesized Sm,N-CDs.
(a) XPS spetra of Sm,N-CDs (b) Sm high resolution XPS spectrum (c) High resolution XPS survey of C1s (d) O1s high resolution spectrum (e) The N1s high resolution XPS spectrum.

(a) Effect of various excitation wavelength on the Sm,N-CDs fluorescence intensity (b) CIE Plot of Fig. 3a (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, Sm-NCDs with epinephrine. (d) overlap between the Ep absorption spectra and the Sm,N-CD emission spectrum.

(a) Epinephrine induced fluorescence quenching spectra of Sm,N-CDs (b) linear correlation plot of F0/F with conc. of Ep. (c) CIE plot of Fig. 4(a) (d) Sm,N-CDs UV–Vis absorption spectra with the addition of various Ep concentrations.

(a) PL quenching efficiency(%) of Sm,N-CDs with various analytes (b) Interference selectivity plot of Sm,N-CDs (c) TCS-PC decay plot of Sm,N-CDs and Sm,N-CDs with Ep. (d) Smartphone based detection of Ep with Sm,N-CDs.
Graphical representation for the synthesis of Sm,N-CDs, and detection of epinephrine.
HOMO, LUMO energy levels of Sm,N-CDs and Ep.
Real sample analysis of Epinephrine with Sm,N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Samarium, nitrogen co-Doped carbon dots for detection of Epinephrine: Theoretical and experimental

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study we have developed a highly sensitive optical sensor for the detection of epinephrine using carbon dots co-doped with samarium and nitrogen (Sm,N-CDs) with a very low detection limit of 28.1 nM and a broad linear dynamic range from (0–105 nM). Various techniques were employed to characterize the synthesized material, aiming to understand its morphological and physicochemical characteristics. The synergistic effect of the synthesized Sm,N-CDs demonstrated excellent optical performance, high selectivity and photostability. Additionally, good recovery findings were obtained when the sensor’s viability for Epinephrine detection in biological fluid samples that had been spiked was evaluated. In addition, we have developed an smartphones-based sensor to record the solution’s fluorescent color shift as it is being sensed. Using a mobile phone application to examine the green, red, and blue values from these photos, the comparable LOD was found to be 17.21 μM in a (0–90 μM) wide linear range of concentration. This straightforward, affordable, and quick screening device is highly demanding for the on-the-spot identification of the analytes at remote locations where advanced instrumentation is typically unavailable. Density functional theory was used to examine energy, stability, band gap, and how Ep interacted with the Sm,N-CDs nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para: Epinephrine (Ep) is a vital catecholamines and neurotransmitters released by the adrenal gland and also identified as adrenaline (AD) [1]. The central neuronal system, as well as the renal, hormonal, and cardiovascular systems, depend on it for proper operation. Numerous physiological events, including pacing the cardiac muscle, raising blood pressure, and triggering quick relief signals, are caused by Ep [2]. The fluctuation of blood levels of Ep concentration is associated with numerous disorders [3]. The occurrence of certain illnesses may cause variations in the levels of Ep in bodily fluids. Parkinson's disease patients have been discovered to have low levels of Ep. In therapeutic settings, AD is also administered as a medicine to treat myocardial infarction and various allergic reactions [4]. Therefore, creating an easy-to-use approach for precisely and reliably identify EP is crucial. Therefore, it is crucial for medical diagnostics to quickly and accurately quantify Ep in human fluids, including plasma and urine, notably for patients with pheochromocytoma, stress, and Parkinsons' disease [5]. Even though Ep is detected using a variety of conventional methods, including capillary electrophoresis [6,7], fluorescence [8,9], chemiluminescence [10], high performance liquid chromatography [11,12], colorimetric [13] flow injection [14] and electrochemical approaches [15,16] have been reported in current years for the diagnosis of AD, but these approaches present a number of challenges regarding complexity, expense and time commitment.

According to current theories, carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a class of surface-passivated nanosized carbon material that has a characteristic quasi-spherical morphology and is typically less than 10 nm [17,18]. Carbon dots have two key advantages: a huge number of surface groups and robust photoluminescence (PL). The excitation wavelength-dependent PL properties of carbon dots (CDs) can be altered by particle size and surface conditions, such as doping and surface functionalization. [19,20]. CNDs are excellent nanocarriers with excellent hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, easy-to-modify functional groups, and most importantly tunable and adjustable characteristics [21]. Because of their excellent photostability, high biocompatibility, ease of synthesis and ease of surface functionalization, CDs are an emerging star in the field of luminescent materials with a widespread series of applications in photoluminescence detection, optoelectronic devices [22] cancer treatment [23], electroanalytical techniques [24], electrochemiluminescence [25], energy storage and conversion and biomedical imaging [26]. The luminescence mechanism of carbon dots is intricately linked to their size-dependent quantum confinement effects, surface chemistry (including functional groups and defects), and the nature of electronic transitions within the nanoparticles. Understanding and controlling these factors are crucial for tuning the optical properties of CDs for various applications, including bioimaging, sensors, and optoelectronics.

In contemporary science, Rare Earth (RE)-doped CDs are even more significant and are regarded as among the most auspicious materials in the fields of nanochemistry, nanophysics and nanomedicine, etc. RE-doped CDs have unique physicochemical characteristics that outperform those of their bulk equivalents [27]. These characteristics include the ability to maintain line-type spectra and intra-configurational transitions despite changes in shape and size. RE-CDs have emerged as essential constituents of modern materials and high-tech goods, thereby emerging as valuable global strategic resources [28]. Nevertheless, individual RE nanoparticles fall short of meeting the demanding specifications needed for innovative materials that have many functions. The advantages of both rare earth and CDs are combined in RE-CDs hybrids, which exhibit appealing and all-encompassing qualities, particularly luminescence and magnetic-optical imaging capability. Due to its notable characteristics and numerous uses in chemical science, biological analysis, and the biomedical field, rare earth carbon nanodots (RE–CDs) hybrid nanomaterials have garnered increased interest.

Scientists have become more interested in RE luminescent and magnetic materials in recent decades, as they are exceptional. RE fluorescent materials have distinctive line-type emission bands, good photostability, significant Stokes/anti-Stokes shifts and extended luminescent lifetimes due to their special 4f electron configurations [29,30]. Nevertheless, low absorption coefficients and fine absorption bands resulting from parity forbidden 4f transitions severely restrict the luminescence intensity of RE ions during their 4f–4f transitions. Consequently, the designer of RE complexes is a useful tactic to increase their luminescence efficacy, despite the fact that they still have the intrinsic drawbacks of photobleaching and low thermal stability. Instead, RE–CD hybrids have superior luminous stability and better processability than pure molecular RE complexes because the inorganic material CDs have better thermal, chemical, and photostability as well as resistance to photobleaching and blinking. The combination of the best features from both RE and CDs, which enhances both device's performance and broadens the luminescence mode, is the primary benefit of luminous RE–-CDs. The reason for the intense focus on RE-CDs for luminescence applications is their notable attributes, which include easy preparation, excellent photostability, and chemical stability, exceptional water solubility, and biocompatibility. This combination greatly expands the range of possible uses for RE-CDs, including providing more thorough and precise diagnostic data that goes beyond traditional fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [31]. Thus, the “one plus one is greater than two” effect is made possible by the integration of CDs and RE, which also actualizes an increase in luminescence qualities for a variety of applications.

Here, we present a unique and straightforward approach for synthesized novel samarium, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Sm,N-CDs) using citric acid, melamine, and Sm(NO3)3⋅6H2O as reaction precursors. Sm,N-CDs demonstrated exceptional photostability in high-concentration saline solution and prolonged irradiation, making them suitable for application as a fluorescence nanosensor for epinephrine detection (Scheme 1 ). The Sm,N-CDs can also be employed as a colorimetric nanosensor for assisted sensing of epinephrine since the Sm,N-CDs color varied from blue to light green during the sensing of Ep. Finally, the built dual-mode nanosensor was used to detect epinephrine in human urine samples, yielding positive outcomes and opening the door to clinical epinephrine detection using Sm,N-CDs. Additionally, we created a smartphone-based detector for Ep sensing. Theoretical studies were done by using DFT for stability, Energy levels, and interactions of Sm,N-CDs with Ep.

Samarium nitrate hexahydrate, melamine, citric acid, histamine, tryptophan, lysine, alanine, glycine, sodium chloride and calcium chloride. were purchased from loba India and epinephrine, serotonin, melatonin, dopamine was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Without additional purification, all chemical reagents were utilized immediately and were of analytical grade or higher. Utilizing ultrapure water from a molecular purification system, all aqueous solutions were made.

The High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) were used to identify the surface particle size and morphology of Sm,N-CDs. The topographical information and thickness of Sm,N-CDs were obtained with a scanning electron microscope (Agilent 5500). The crystallinity of Sm,N-CDs was examined with X rays diffractometer (XRD). Moreover, an X ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) from Physical Electronics was employed to characterize the chemical species of Sm,N-CDs based nano-materials and binding energy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra was observed using Lab-india UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of Sm,N-CDs were noted using fluorescence spectrophotometer in the excitation wavelength ranges from 300 to 400. The surface functional groups of Sm,N-CDs were identified by using FT-IR spectrophotometer and zeta potential in the dynamic light scattering (DLS). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime was calculated using time correlated single photon counts (TCSPC). Gaussian16 software was used for DFT calculations and RGB color detector software for smartphone based detection of Ep.

Gaussian16 software was utilized to conduct a theoretical investigation (DFT) on the structure of N-CDs, Sm,N-CDs, and the interaction between Ep and Sm,N-CDs. For theoretical investigations on N-CDs and epinephrine, the 631G(d,p) basis sets were utilized; however, for Samarium, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots SDD basic sets applied. GAUSSIAN16 was used to calculate electronic structures, total energy, optimization, HOMO and LUMO.
Step1. Synthesis of samarium nitride nanosheets.

Dissolved 1 g of citric acid, 0.3 g melamine and 0.3 g samarium nitrate in 15 ml of distilled water by stirring on magnetic stirrer for 2 h at 6000 rpm then transferred into autoclave and put in hydrothermal oven at 180 °C upto 14 h. The obtained product in the autoclave was washed with ethanol, and dry in oven to obtain the solid powder of the Samarium nitride nanosheets.
Step2. Synthesis of samarium, Nitrogen co-doped carbon dots.

0.5 g of samarium nitride nanosheets and 1 g of citric acid dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water then put on magnetic stirrer for 1 h at 6000 rpm. Then the obtained suspension was transferred into autoclave and put in muffle furnace at 180 °C for 4 h further dissolved in distilled water, centrifuging (6000 rpm, 20 mins) and transfer in dialysis membrane (24 h) for removing large particles.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (λex at 340 nm) was used to observe the fluorescence spectra and intensities when various concentrations of Ep were mixed to the Sm,N-CDs solution (10−6M). For quality control, the experiment was conducted twice. Room temperature was used for the Ep detection process. In order to examine the nano-sensing system's selectivity for Ep further, several amino acids and neurotransmitters were substituted by Ep and added to the Sm,N-CDs solution under identical conditions. Simultaneously, the sensor system's anti-interference performance was assessed. To quantify the fluorescence intensities, several interferents (100 nM) were first added one at a time into the Sm,N-CDs aqueous solution then adding Ep (50 nM) to the mixture mentioned above, the fluorescence emission spectra and intensities were noted.

The Sm,N-CDs were prepared by two step process using hydrothermal and muffle furnace treatment of Sm(NO3)3·6H2O, melamine and citric acid at 180 °C for 14 h in hydrothermal then at 180 °C for 4 h in furnace. The TEM result, as displayed in Fig. 1 a, demonstrates that the Sm,N-CDs are evenly distributed across the ultra-thin carbon film-coated grid. ImageJ software was used to determine the Sm,N-CDs average particle size that is 8–9 nm shown in Fig. 1e. SEM image of Sm,N-CDs were shown in Fig. 1b This demonstrates that particles are round and exist at the nanoscale. AFM image of the Sm,N-CDs were respectively depicted in Fig. 1c, displaying that the Sm,N-CDs possess a narrow height dispersal with a average height lower than 7–8 nm. Energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) of the Sm,N-CDs is shown in Fig.S1. It can be seen that the Sm,N-CDs are composed of C, O, N and Sm elements with the Sm content of 15.74 wt%, C content 34.55 wt%, N content 8.05 wt% and O content of 41.66 wt%. These findings show that the Sm,N-CDs, a novel class of CDs with superior structure, have plentiful Sm, good dispersion and a spherical-like structure. Fig. 1d shows the XRD spectra of synthesized Sm,N-CDs and N-CDs that proves the amorphous behaviour of CDs and peaks at 2Theta 280 and 230 respectively. The elemental analysis, FTIR and XPS spectra were carefully examined in order to look into the surface functional groups, chemical alignments and binding energy of the produced Sm,N-CDs.

Fig. 1f shows the FT-IR spectra that was utilized to verify the chemical conformation and external structure of the Sm,N-CDs. The O–H or N–H stretching vibrations are responsible for the noticeable vibration peak observed in the Sm,N-CDs at around 3319 cm−1 [32]. The C–H stretching vibration is linked to the vibration peaks between 2974 and 2877 cm−1. The strong band at 1451 cm−1 and the absorption peak at around 1647 cm−1 are linked to the vibration that is due to C=C and C=O, respectively, confirming the presence of the hydrophilic groups on Sm,N-CDs surface [33]. Furthermore, the distinct absorption band at roughly 1379 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of C–N [34]. The stretching vibrations peaks of C–C and C-O are linked to the faint peaks in the 1050–1150 cm−1 range. The stretching vibrations of Sm-O and Sm3+ were correlated with the unique absorption band at 877 cm−1 and 1045 cm− 1 [35]. All of the findings demonstrate that Sm and N were successfully doped into the Sm,N-CDs, which also have a large number of functional groups.

The presence of Sm(3d), C(1 s), N(1 s), and O(1 s) is further indicated by the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a). The Sm(3d) spectra (Fig. 2b) reveal the existence of two peaks at 1110.32 eV and 1082.8 eV, respectively. These peaks correspond to the energy level of Sm3+ which is a spin–orbit linked Sm3d3/2 and Sm3d5/2 [36,37]. The existence of C=O and C=C is indicated by the two prominent peaks in the high-scan spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2c) at 289.2 and 284.9 eV, respectively. One peak for C=O can be found at 532.28 eV in the O1s spectra (Fig. 2d). N–H, C=N, and N-C are the binding energy bands of the N1s spectra of the Sm,N-CDs (Fig. 2e) at 401.6, 400.21, and 398.2 eV, respectively [38–40]. Using XPS data, the oxidation state of Sm is determined to be + 3.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the Sm,N-CDs were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrofluorometer respectively, to examine the photosensitive properties of the co-doped CDs. Under the light of a UV lamp, the Sm,N-CDs exhibit vivid cyan fluorescence when the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, and a strong fluorescence emission peak appears at 420 nm. A feature of the emission that is dependent on the excitation in the 300–400 nm excitation wavelength range (Fig. 3 a). The CIE map (Fig. 3b), which is in line with the fluorescence spectra of Fig. 3a, provided clarification on the luminescence color variation of the Sm,N-CDs. The developed Sm,N-CDs UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibits a peak at approximately 290 nm. These are attributed to the aromatic C––C bonds π → π* transition and the n → π* transition of the C––N and C––O bonds, respectively as shown in Fig. 3c. These transitions associate with the Sm,N-CDs on surface states and are in charge of the fluorescence behaviour of Sm,N-CDs.

The as-synthesised Sm,N-CDs fluorescence quantum yield (QY) in the aqueous phase is found to be 70 % using quinine sulphate as a reference. (Fig.S2) and the QY of the synthesisied N-CDs is less than Sm,N-CDs that is 51.4 %. The overlay of absorption spectra of Ep and excitation spectra of Sm,N-CDs shown in Fig.S3a. The Sm,N-CDs have a large number of various functional groups, and all of the data demonstrate that Sm was successfully doped. The zeta potential (ζ) value of Sm,N-CDs is −18.6 mV, as can be seen in Fig.S3b. Because there are several functional groups, the Sm,N-CDs have a negative charge, which increases their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solutions.

Good stability and exceptional properties are prerequisites for expanding the usage of Sm,N-CDs. Under UV lamp radiation, high saline solution, With various pH and after many days the stability of the Sm,N-CDs was assessed. Examined was the effect of UV lamp irradiation on the behaviour of fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of the Sm,N-CDs has not changed noticeably when exposed UV lamp for 120 min (Fig.S4d), indicating that the Sm,N-CDs have a better resistance to photobleaching. The Sm,N-CDs fluorescence intensity was observed at various NaCl concentrations, and the results showed that they show outstanding stability at high ionic strength settings (2.0 M, Fig.S4a), which has a great advantage in various environmental and sensing applications. Additionally, the fluorescence stability of the Sm,N-CDs in various pH solutions was examined; the findings are displayed in Fig.S4b. Initially, in the acidic range, fluorescence Intensity is low, but when we raise the pH of the solution, the intensity increase continuously, and Sm,N-CDs fluorescence intensity has high at pH 11–12; further, it decreases with a rise in pH from 12 to 14, suggesting that they have excellent fluorescence in basic medium. According to every finding, Sm,N-CDs offer a great deal of promise for use in a diversity of industries, as well as biosensing and environmental monitoring. After two months, we saw that the fluorescence intensity of Sm,N-CDs remained constant (Fig. S4c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building the nanosensor based on Sm,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We looked into the use of Sm,N-CDs in the arena of fluorescence sensors further because of their good photostability and fluorescence characteristics.. The high fluorescence intensity of Sm,N-CDs at 420 nm decreases gradually when varying quantities of Ep (0–105 nM) were applied, as indicated in Fig. 4 a. Hence, a fluorescence turn of nanosensor designed for the sensing of Ep with high accuracy and reliability. According to the CIE chromaticity map (Fig. 4c), the fluorescence color of Sm,N-CDs shifted from blue to green with an increase in conc. of Ep, which is consistent with the fluorescence quenching spectra in Fig. 4a.

Fig. 4b represents the titration method as well as the linear relationship between the Sm,N-CDs, and Ep. With an R2 value of 0.9857, the fluorescence ratio (F0/F) and Ep concentration exhibit a strong linear correlation in the 0–105 nM range. Additionally, the Limit of detection (LOD) for Ep that can be detected is 28.1 nM, which is substantially less than other current approaches. Table S1 compares the as-constructed nano-sensor with the published analytical method for Ep, indicating that our method is equivalent to previous existing methods with a minimum detection limit and a larger linear range. The Sm,N-CDs UV–Vis spectra were examined subsequent to the incorporation of diverse Ep concentrations (0–60 nM) into the Sm,N-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 4d. The absorption band at 290 nm was considerably increased gradually with the addition of Ep. All findings demonstrate that the constructed “turn- off” fluorescence and colorimetry nanosensor has excellent selectivity, strong anti-interference properties and exceptional sensitivity for the correct finding of Ep in real sample analysis.

Generally, potential quenching mechanisms include the inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), static quenching effect (SQE), and dynamic quenching effect (DQE). Among others, the IFE and FRET mechanism typically requires a significant spectral overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of a quencher and the emission or excitation spectrum of a fluorescent agent. It is commonly recognized that in spectrofluorometric, IFE is typically taken into account as a potential source of inaccuracy. It's interesting to note that IFE has been used successfully to transform analytical absorption results into fluorescence signals, making target analyte detection easy and straightforward [41,42]. However, the usage of IFE based fluorescence assess is typically limited to a certain degree due to the limited selection of appropriate fluorophore and absorber. This research uses a novel fluorescence sensor for the detection of Ep based on inner filter effects.

The fluorescence sensing mechanism of Ep using Sm,N-CDs was thoroughly investigated and verified. Originally, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ep and the fluorescence emission spectrum of Sm,N-CDs were presented in order to explain the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Sm,N-CDs generated by Ep (Fig. 3d). Notably, there was an indication of the likelihood of IFE and FRET since the emission spectrum of Sm,N-CDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Ep overlapped. FRET and IFE mechanisms further differentiate by observing the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore. More specifically, a fluorophore's fluorescence lifetime diminutions for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), whereas it remains constant for IFE [43–46]. To gain deeper insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Sm,N-CDs induced by Ep, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was employed. Fig. 5 c illustrates the fluorescence decay profile, revealing that Sm,N-CDs exhibited a lifetime of 4.8 ns. Importantly, this lifetime remained unchanged upon the addition of EP, suggesting that Ep did not diminish the fluorescence lifetime. This observation aligns with the observed fluorescence quenching effect. The quenching mechanism is likely facilitated by an IFE process from Sm,N-CDs to Ep [47,48]. This further confirmed that Ep-induced fluorescence quenching behaviour was mostly caused by IFE rather than FRET. It is easy to accomplish the simple, quick, accurate, and highly sensitive screening of Ep activity by utilizing the above-mentioned IFE-based method.

Density functional theory was used for theoretical studies; herein, we found the overall energy of N-carbon dots and the effects of co-doping on the energy, HOMO, LUMO, and stability (Figs. S6–S14). We observed that initially for N-CDs energy is −98,598.6 eV and for samarium, nitrogen co-doped CDs, the total energy of Sm,N-CDs is −102832 eV that decreased it means stability of co-doped carbon dots increase. Then we calculate the HOMO, LUMO energies of both N-CDs and Sm,N-CDs we found that energy band gap 0.5859 eV and 0.131 eV respectively. With the theoretical method, we have premeditated the stability of developed materials. The DFT calculation results further support the quantum size effect of CDs by showing that the band gap energy gradually fell from 0.5859 eV to 0.131 eV as the particle size grew. They proposed that the decrease in band gap brought about by the π electrons delocalization with the larger CDs size according to literature the band gap decreased after doping was applied to CDs, which helped to increase the fluorescence of CDs (Fig.S5). The decrease in bandgap in Sm,N-CDs enhances luminescence primarily by increasing the population of excited states available for radiative recombination and by improving the efficiency of emission processes. Unlike traditional semiconductor nanocrystals, the emission spectrum of carbon dots does not exhibit significant shifts with bandgap changes, highlighting the unique luminescence mechanism based on surface states and defect levels. Hence Sm,N-CDs are more stable and high fluorescence than N-CDs due to decrease in band gap and energy. Moreover, we also studied the interactions of Ep with Sm,N-CDs and calculated the energy, Homo, LUMO and band gap. We observed that energy of Sm,N-CDs + Ep was −103284 eV and band gap was 0.33 eV. The energy of Sm,N-CDs + Ep is lower than pure Sm,N-CDs that proves that complex are highly stable [49–52].

With the helps of DFT we can also prooves the sensing mechanism for epinephrine using Sm,N-CDs. Our experimental results gave us an idea of Inner filter effects mechanism for the quenching. Here in also we found that Homo, Lumo energy of Sm,N-CDs is lower than that of epinephrine so there is no chance of transfer of electron from Homo of Sm,N-CDs to Lumo of Ep hence, the sensing mechanism is IFE, Hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions shown in scheme2 .

Selective sensing is always essential for applications in the biotic and environmental arenas. This study investigated the selectivity of Sm,N-CDs using optical sensing and changes in fluorescence spectra with the addition of other species. For our research objects, we selected a variety of amino acids and neurotransmitters, including histamine, tryptophan, lysine, alanine, glycine, epinephrine, serotonin, melatonin, dopamine, sodium, calcium, sulphide, and chloride. As shown in Fig. 5, only Ep is able to significantly quench the fluorescence spectra of the Sm,N-CDs, demonstrating that the Sm,N-CDs have good selectivity towards Ep. In the end, the process of determining Ep may be impacted by additional potential interferents, so it is important to investigate the Sm,N-CDs ability to prevent interference. The fluorescence intensity of Sm,N-CDs remains largely unaffected in the presence of interferences at a concentration of 100 nM. However, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly upon the addition of Ep (50 nM) to the mixture indicating that the Sm,N-CDs are a good anti-interference tool for detecting Ep. The PL quenching efficiency of Sm,N-CDs when exposed to 100 nM of 12 distinct species is depicted in Fig. 5a and shows higher PL quenching efficiency with epinephrine rather than any other species. The Sm,N-CDs also detect Ep in the presence of various interfering species shown in Fig. 5b. Table S1 also compares a few alternative carbon quantum dot-based materials for Ep detection with our suggested approach utilizing Sm,N-CDs materials. When compared to the results of the literature, it is clear that our material is highly effective for the detection of Ep.

Under UV-light, the Sm,N-CDs exhibit a continuous variation in color from blue Gray to light green when the concentration of Ep increases from 0 to 90 μM. Thus, Ep can be identified using a smartphone (Fig. 5d). After adding varying doses (0–90 μM) of Ep to the Sm,N-CDs solution, pictures were captured in UV-Light using a smartphone. By using an iPhone equipped with a color scanning application (RGB), it was possible to swiftly extract the RGB (Red, Green, Blue) color model (B, G and R channels) of images [53–55]. To assess how the absorbance intensities of the photographs changed with varying Ep contents, the value of channel (G+B)/2 was computed. The (G+B)/2 channel and the Ep concentrations have a strong proportional association (R2 = 0.9916), as seen in Fig. 5d, and the LOD is 17.21 μM It allows semi-quantitative Ep detection to be performed using just a portable smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reliability for real analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of the fluorometry nanosensor was established using the conventional addition experiment, which involves spiking various amounts of epinephrine in human urine. Prior to examination, the urine samples underwent filtration using a microporous membrane to eliminate any particle suspensions. Following this, we were preserved at 4 °C in anticipation of additional analysis. Relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.258–2.839 (n = 4) are obtained with the desired recoveries between 97.06 % and 104.62 %, as shown in Table 1 , indicating that the fluorometric nano-sensor has the potential to be reliable for the detection of Ep in real samples. These findings demonstrate that the nanosensor is suitable for the detection of epinephrine in comicated biologic samples and possesses high selectivity, outstanding repeatability and great anti interference performance. The accuracy of the detection findings can be increased by using the UV modes to mutually verify the results.

In conclusion, we have created an efficient nanosensor based on Sm,N-CDs that can detect the neurotransmitter epinephrine with low LODs, excellent selectivity, and sensitivity. In a hydrothermal and muffle furnace, samarium nitrate, melamine, and citric acid are combined to create Sm,N-CDs without the use of harsh acids or bases, hazardous solvents, or other chemicals. It is anticipated that this procedure will work well for biological applications. By controlling the experimental conditions throughout the detection process, we observed selective detection with a lower limit of detection of 28.1 nM and heralded linear correlations spanning from 0-105 nM. Hydrogen bonds are created between the Ep-Sm,N-CDs surface ligand and we were able to identify this interaction and sensing mechanism for detection of Ep with Sm,N-CDs using DFT. Additionally, we created a smartphone-based sensor to identify Ep. Our research creates new opportunities for the use of Sm,N-CDs in biomedical applications, medication creation, and early neurological disease diagnosis and detection.

Jyoti Duhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sangeeta Obrai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2024.07.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
